title: 2 4 Level surfaces
body:
2.4 LEVEL SURFACES, TANGENT PLANES & NORMAL LINES

The level surface of a scalar field φ(x, y, z) is a surface formed by all the points (x, y, z) with the same value of φ. In two dimensions this is called the level curve.

The level surface corresponding to the value c of the scalar field is described by the equation φ(x, y, z) = c. At every point on the level surface, the gradient of φ is perpendicular to the level surface.

The tangent plane to the surface φ(x, y, z) = c at the point P (x0 , y0 , z0 ) is given by

∇φ|P · [(x − x0 ) i + (y − y0 ) j + (z − z0 ) k] = 0.

The normal line to the surface φ(x, y, z) = c at the point P (x0 , y0 , z0 ) is given by

r(t) = r0 + ∇φ|P t

where r(t) = x(t)i + y(t)j + z(t)k, r0 = x0 i + y0 j + z0 k and t is the distance along the normal line measured from the point P .

Example 1 Consider the temperature field T (x, y, z) = x2 + y 2 + z 2 . Find the temperature at the point P (3, 0, 4) and describe the surface of all points whose temperature is the same as that of P (This is called a level surface).

F T = 25 and the level surface is all points on a sphere of radius 5 F

Example 2 Consider the two-dimensional temperature field T (x, y) = x2 + y 2 . Find the direction and magnitude of maximum increase and maximum decrease of T at the point P (3, 3). Describe the level curve at P and verify graphically that the direction of maximum increase of T at P is perpendicular to the level curve through P .

√ F 6i + 6j, −6i − 6j, 72 F

5x2 Example 3 Consider the scalar field φ(x, y) = in R2 . Find the direction and y magnitude of maximum increase of φ at the point P (2, 20). Describe the level curve at P and verify graphically that the direction of maximum increase of φ at P is perpendicular to the level curve through P .

q 1 401 F i − 20 j, 400 F

In the previous two examples we have seen that the gradient ∇φ at a point P is perpendicular to the level surface (or curve in 2-D) at that point. In fact, this is always the case — at every point on a level surface φ(x, y, z) = c, the gradient of φ is perpendicular to the level surface. We will prove this fascinating little result the next Topic.

The gradient can also be used to define the tangent plane and normal line to a surface. Let us first recall how to define planes in three dimensions.

Example 4 Find the Cartesian equation of the plane perpendicular to n = −18i+11j−k and passing through the point P (2, 0, 5)

F 18x − 11y + z = 41 F

Example 5 Find the tangent plane and the normal line to the surface

x4 + y 4 + 3z 4 = 20

at the point P (2, 1, −1).

      x 2 32 F 32x + 4y − 12z = 80, y  =  1  +  4  t : t ∈ R. F z −1 −12

Figure 1: Example 7: Tangent plane and normal line to the surface x4 + y 4 + 3z 4 = 20 at the point P (2, 1, −1).

An Intuitive Verification of the Method of Lagrange (Optional)

Recall that the method of Lagrange Multipliers states that to find the local minima and maxima of f (x, y) subject to the constraint g(x, y) = 0 we find the values of x, y and λ that simultaneously satisfy the equations ∂f ∂g ∂f ∂g −λ = 0, −λ = 0, together with g(x, y) = 0. ∂x ∂x ∂y ∂y

We can now justify this quite easily:

The constraint g(x, y) = 0 is simply a level curve for the scalar field g(x, y). Pick a point P on the curve g(x, y) = 0. At P , grad(g) will point in the direction of maximal increase of g(x, y) and will also point perpendicular to the level curve g(x, y) = 0.

P

grad(g) grad(f)

g(x,y)=0

However g(x, y) = 0 is not a level curve for f (x, y). Thus grad(f ) will most likely not point perpendicular to g(x, y) = 0. Since grad(f ) points in the direction of maximum increase of f (x, y) it will be possible to increase f (x, y) by sliding a little around the level curve in the direction suggested by grad(f ).

If the point P is sitting at a maximum value of f (x, y) then there will be no component of grad(f ) in the tangential direction through which f (x, y) could be increased. In other words if P is positioned so that f (x, y) is maximised (or minimised),then grad(f ) will also point perpendicular to g(x, y) = 0. This means that grad(f ) and grad(g) are parallel vectors and hence that grad(f ) = λgrad(g). This means that

∂f ∂f ∂g ∂g i+ j = λ( i + j) ∂x ∂y ∂x ∂y and so ∂f ∂g ∂f ∂g =λ , =λ ∂x ∂x ∂y ∂y as required.

You can now do Q49 and 55
